content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To    : Opinion Request Meeting Participants                Date : March 24, 1982

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Bob Leidigh

Subject: Public Generally - Yolo County/Capay Valley;
         Westlake Village

     There is a planning commissioner in Yolo County who resides
in the Capay Valley, a region in the northwest portion of the
County. The Valley is very rural in character and agriculture is
its livelihood. The Planning Commission is redoing the County
General Plan, in segments. One segment is the Capay Valley Region.
Because he is the only planning commissioner who resides in the
area, the other members of the Planning Commission desire his
participation. We have been contacted for telephone advice as to
whether or not he has a conflict requiring him to not participate.

     The Capay Valley Region contains some 108,000 acres, occupied
by a scant 675 people. There are 467 individual owners of real
property, plus 6 governmental entities which own real property.
There are 1,069 separate parcels with a total of 397 housing
units, 91 of which are nonfarm properties. Thus, approximately
300 of the dwelling units are situated on farms. Of the 1,069
parcels, 860 are zoned agricultural, permitting a minimum lot
size of 5 acres; however, very few parcels are less than 20 acres,
the minimum size permissible for a dwelling on ag land. The
remaining 118 parcels are either government-owned or other industrial/
commercial.

     The planning commissioner in question owns 160 acres of
range land, which he has leased to a tenant. He also has a 1/3
interest in another 900 acres of range land, also leased to this
same tenant. Both leases are 1-year leases which have been renewed
annually and will be so renewed in the future. He manages and
controls 500 acres owned by his father which is leased to another
tenant. He also manages and controls another 100 acres owned by
his father, which he farms himself. He gets a salary plus a
percentage of the profits on this latter parcel. Some of the
foregoing land is on the Valley floor, some is hilly and some in
the mountains.

     The thrust of the General Plan proposal is to preserve the
agricultural use of the land and to direct residential development
into the two small towns (Capay and Guinda).

Opinion Request Meeting Participants
March 24, 1982
Page Two


County Counsel advises that we assume the following: (1)
The decisions involving the General Plan will have a material
financial effect upon the land owned, managed and controlled by
the planning commissioner. (2) The effect upon that land will
not be distinguishable from the effect on the "public generally"
if the general public is considered to be Capay Valley rather
than Yolo County.

What advice? Entire County is Publicly Generally

Westlake Village - Proposed Rent Control Ordinance

I am also working on another, written, advice request, which
involves a potential public generally issue. I expect to meet
with lawyers from "both sides" next week and wish some guidance
from you so as to best focus my inquiry at that time.

Westlake Village is a recently incorporated city in the
northwest corner of Los Angeles County. It has a population of
11,500. There are 2,370 housing units, of which 160 are mobilehomes,
situated in Oak Forest Mobilehome Park, and 234 are apartments
located in one complex (the only one in town), of which 115 units
are occupied by a total of 200 residents. Therefore, the number
of housing units affected by the proposed rent control ordinance
is: (1) 16.6%, if you count all rental housing units; or (2)
11.7%, if you only count occupied rental housing units (i.e.,
rental households). Both of these figures exceed the percentage
in my letter to Paul Morgan (copy attached). In the Owen Opinion,
2 PPPC Opinions 77, the Commission found that: "we conclude that
residential home owners within and in the immediate vicinity of
the 'core area' constitute a 'significant segment' of the public."
A check with the City of Davis Planning Department shows that, at
the time of the Owen Opinion (6/76), there were 11,729 housing
units in Davis and 382 residential home owners (owner occupied
dwellings) "within and in the vicinity of the 'core area'." This
is a percentage of only 3.25%. There was some allusion to the
possibility that all homeowners in Davis would be equally affected,
but that has not been borne out by time as the "core area" has
since been expanded to include some of the "vicinity" surrounding
it, thus affecting those homeowners differently. In addition, in
Owen, the Commission found 250 retail merchants (less than 1% of
the population of the city) to constitute a "significant segment"
of the public generally.

The "other side" would have us treat the mobilehome park
separately from the apartment complex, on the ground that the
proposed rent control ordinance would affect mobilehome park
residents differently than apartment dwellers. In that case, the
percentage of Westlake Village households which are mobilehomes
would be 6.8%.

Opinion Request Meeting Participants
March 24, 1982
Page Three


See the two attached advice letters for further parameters.
In addition to the Owen opinion, there are the Orlesby (1 FPPC
Opinions 71 at 81) and Brown (4 PPPC Opinions 19 at 23) opinions,
as well as my letter to Raymond Haight (10/16/82, No. A-81-12-
509) if anyone wishes to delve more deeply into the issue.

BL:plh:33-8
Attachments

State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660         322-5901        322-6441         322-6464


July 31, 1980


Leonard Rutledge, President
J & R Enterprises
411 North Second Street
Blythe, CA  92225

Dear Mr. Rutledge:

     This is in response to your letter of June 30, 1980.
The Political Reform Act, which this agency administers,
allows us to make a public analysis of how the law applies
to a particular set of facts in only two situations: when
we are responding to a request for an opinion or advice from
someone concerning his own duties or obligations under the
Act, see Government Code Section 83114;1/ or when we are
responding to a formal or informal complaint requesting that
we initiate an enforcement action. As you are not inquiring
about yourself, I cannot offer you an opinion or advice, but
the following general discussion of the law may help you
decide whether or not you want to file a complaint with the
Enforcement Division of this agency.

     The Political Reform Act provides that no public official
shall make, participate in making or in any way use his
official position to influence a governmental decision if he
knows or has reason to know that the decision will have a
foreseeable material financial effect, distinguishable from
its effect on the public generally, on a business which
employs him or in which he has an investment, on a source of
income to him, or on real property in which he has an interest.
See Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103. Under regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission which interpret

_________________
1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code
unless otherwise noted.

Leonard Rutledge, President
July 31, 1980
Page Two


these sections of the Act, a "material" financial effect is
a significant effect, and in determining whether or not an
effect on a business is significant, consideration should be
given to whether or not it will increase or decrease:

     (A)  The annualized gross revenues by the lessor of:

          1.  One hundred thousand dollars ($100,000); or

          2.  One percent if it is one thousand dollars
     ($1,000) or more; or

     (B)  Annual net income by the lessor of:

          1.  Fifty thousand dollars ($50,000); or

          2.  One half of one percent if it is one thousand
     dollars ($1,000) or more; or

     (C)  Current assets or liabilities by the lessor of:

          1.  One hundred thousand dollars ($100,000); or

          2.  One half of one percent if it is one thousand
     dollars ($1,000) or more.

     Current assets are deemed to be decreased by the
amount of any expenses incurred as a result of a govern-
mental decision.

                    2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(1)

     Assuming that a decision will have a material financial
effect on one of a public official's financial interests,
the next question is whether that effect is distinguishable
from the effect the decision will have on the public gener-
ally, which includes any significant segment of the public
generally under the provisions of the Commission's regulation
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703. Although in the case of a
city or county one generally considers all the people living
within the geographical boundaries of the city or county as
being the "public" and works from that point in determining
what a significant segment of the public is, the situation
may be different in a special district. If only a limited
amount of land is taxed to raise revenues for the district,
or if the district is financed by user fees and only users
vote in district elections, the concept of who constitutes

Leonard Rutledge, President
July 31, 1980
Page Three

the "public" may be quite different. In any case, this
Commission has used a very general mark of approximately 30
percent of the public as constituting a "significant segment"
of it.

If an official has in fact participated in a decision
in which he has a financial interest, the extent of available
remedies depends on whether or not the official is designated
in a Conflict of Interest Code covering his agency. If so,
the participation should be a violation of the Code as well
as a violation of Section 87100 of the Act itself. Violation
of the Code may be a misdemeanor subjecting the violator to,
among other penalties, a fine of up to $10,000. Section
91000. Civil penalties may be available against a designated
employee in the amount of up to three times the value of any
economic benefit he realized as the result of the violation
of the disqualification provision of his Conflict of Interest
Code. Section 91005(b). This agency is empowered issue
administrative remedies with respect to violations of Conflict
of Interest Codes, including fines of up to $2,000 per viola-
tion. Section 83116. Finally, upon a determination by a
court that a violation of either Section 87100 or the dis-
qualification provision of a Conflict of Interest Code has
occurred, and that the official action in relation to which
the violation occurred might not otherwise have been taken,
the court may set the official action aside as void. In
doing so, however, the court must accord due weight to any
injury that may be suffered by innocent persons relying on
the official action. Section 91003(b).

Enclosed for your review is a package of materials
concerning filing a complaint with this agency. If you have
any questions about these materials, please contact our
Enforcement Division.

Sincerely,

Sarah Cameron
Deputy Chief for
Conflicts of Interest
Legal Division

STC:kp

Enclosure

bcc: Bob Blasier

State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660           322-5901          322-6441           322-6444

December 2, 1981

Paul H. Morgan
City Attorney of Westminster
5660 Newport Center Drive, Suite 295
Newport Beach, CA  92660

Re: Your Request for Advice
     on Behalf of Evar Peterson

Dear Mr. Morgan:

     On November 2, 1981, you telephoned the Fair Political
Practices Commission seeking written confirmation of oral,
telephone advice which you received in April 1980 relative
to a possible conflict of interest of City Councilman Evar
Peterson. The question arose in the context of consideration
of an ordinance for mediation of rent increases in mobile
home parks. Because of a turnover in staff during the intervening
year and one-half, we are unable to verify that such advice
was given to you at that time. On November 3, 1981, you
wrote to the Commission seeking advice on a possible future
decision by Mr. Peterson involving essentially identical
facts.

     Based upon the facts you have supplied to us relating
to your April 1980 telephone advice request, I can confirm
that our advice to you at that time would have been that
Mr. Peterson had no conflict of interest requiring his dis-
qualification. At that time, we would have advised you that
his mobile home park tenancy did not constitute an "interest
in real property" within the meaning of the Political Reform
Act. We also would have advised you that if he did have
such an interest, the effect of the ordinance, if any, upon
such an interest would not be distinguishable from its effect
upon a "significant segment of the public generally." Either
of these conclusions, standing alone, would suffice to negate
a conflict of interest on Mr. Peterson's part.

     However, in March 1981 the Commission adopted its opinion
in the case of Martin Overstreet (Overstreet, 6 FPPC Opinions

Paul H. Morgan
December 2, 1981
Page Two


12. No. 80-010, March 12, 1981), which altered the Commission's
interpretation on the issue of whether a month-to-month
tenancy could constitute an "interest in real property" for
disqualification purposes under the Act. Post-Overstreet,
our advice would have changed as to whether Mr. Peterson had
an interest in real property. However, there has been no
change in our interpretation of what constitutes the "public
generally."

For the record, the material facts relating to Mr. Peterson's
decision, as provided to us by you, are as set forth below.
The advice which I have stated that we would have given is
based upon these facts.

     1. Councilman Peterson is a tenant in a mobile home
park in Westminster.

     2. Councilman Peterson owns his mobile home and rents
the space on which it is located on a month-to-month basis
for $255.36 per month. This is the general level for space
rentals in Westminster mobile home parks.

     3. Pursuant to California law [Civil Code Section
798.55(b)], it requires a 60 day notice to terminate such a
mobile home park tenancy (his mobile home is of sufficient
size to come within this statutory provision).

     4. Pursuant to California Law [Civil Code Sections
799.55(b) and 798.56], eviction may only be "for a reason
specified" in that law ("for cause" eviction).

     5. Westminster consists of 24,220 dwelling units; of
those, 2,642 are mobile homes situated in mobile home parks.
The vacancy rate in mobile home parks is virtually nil; the
vacancy rate overall for Westminster is 2.14%. Thus, mobile
home households constitute 10.9% or more of the households
in Westminster.

     6. There are 17 mobile home parks in Westminster, with
a population of 4,791; the total population of Westminster
is 71,133. Thus, mobile home dwellers constitute 6.7% of
the total Westminster population.

Government Code Section 87100_1/ provides:

_________________________
     1/ All statutory references made are to the Government
Code unless otherwise noted.

Paul H. Morgan
December 2, 1981
Page Three


     No public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in any
way attempt to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason
to know he has a financial interest.

     Section 87103 provides further, in pertinent part, as
follows:

          An official has a financial interest in a decision
     within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
     foreseeable that the decision will have a material
     financial effect distinguishable from its effect on the
     public generally, on:

                                    * * *

          (b) Any real property in which the public
     official has a direct or indirect interest worth
     more than one thousand dollars ($1,000)....

     The question of whether or not the decision would have
an effect on Mr. Peterson which would be distinguishable
from its effect on the "public generally" is determinative
in this type of situation. Our regulation, 2 Cal. Admin. Code
Section 18703 refines this term to include "a significant
segment of the public generally." While that phrase has
never been quantified by the Commission, it has been satisfied
by such segments as (1) owners of less than four units of
residential rental property, in Los Angeles;2/ (2) all tenants,
in Berkeley;3/ (3) homeowners, in Davis;4/ and shopowners
who lease their shop space, in Davis.5/

     A recurring analysis applied by the Commission to any
segment in question has been the extent to which that segment
is "ade up of diverse members, whose only common bond" is
their membership in this grouping.6/ Turning to the segment
in question here, mobile home owners residing in mobile home

     2/ Ferraro, 4 FPPC Opinions 62, No. 73-009, November 7,
1978 (copy attached).
     3/ Overstreet, supra.
     4/ Owen, 2 FPPC Opinions 77, No. 75-005, June 2, 1976
(copy attached).
     5/ Id.
     6/ Ferraro, supra, at 56 and Overstreet, supra, at 17-
18.

Paul H. Morgan
December 2, 1981
Page Four


parks in Westminster, these households make up 10.9% or more
of all households in Westminster.— Mobile home owners
are members of virtually all occupations and are similarly
diverse in their other characteristics. Our advice would
have been that mobile home owners in Westminster's 17 parks
constituted a significant segment of the public generally.

However, if the effect of Mr. Peterson's decision was
distinguishable from its effect on other mobile home owners,
then a conflict would exist. The ordinance on its face
affects all mobile home owners alike, subject to the future,
contingent actions of groups of park residents, "Park Committees,"
and arbitrators, as set forth in the ordinance. As the
Commission held in Overstreet, supra, so long as the ordinance
adoption decision affected all the mobile home owners (as
opposed to only his park, or himself, etc.), then there
would have been no conflict of interest for Mr. Peterson.8/

We regret that we were unable to confirm that we gave
you this advice in the past. However, we are confident that
this is the advice which would have been given to you at
that time based upon these facts. In the future, you may
wish to obtain our written advice pursuant to Section 83114(b).
In the meantime, if I can be of any further assistance,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Robert E. Leidigh
Counsel
Legal Division

REL:plh
Attachments


1/ The vacancy rate for mobile home spaces is smaller
than for other housing units; consequently, the actual percentage
of households is slightly higher than the direct ratio of
spaces to dwelling units. Mobile home owners constitute
6.7% of the population of Westminster; however, population
was not the basis for the Commission's analysis in Owen.

8/ Overstreet, supra at 18; and Owen, supra at 31.

sections.question_synthetic: The document addresses two primary legal questions: (1) whether a planning commissioner in Yolo County with substantial agricultural land interests in the Capay Valley has a conflict of interest requiring disqualification from participating in the General Plan update for that region, and (2) guidance on applying the 'public generally' standard to a proposed rent control ordinance in Westlake Village that would affect a specific percentage of housing units.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC memorandum seeks guidance from opinion request meeting participants on how to advise regarding the Capay Valley planning commissioner's potential conflict and how to analyze the 'significant segment of the public' standard in the Westlake Village rent control context, noting that prior opinions have found small percentages (3.25% and less than 1%) to constitute 'significant segments' despite higher percentages in the current matters.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an internal memorandum rather than a formal advice letter response, containing two distinct inquiries and reference materials. The Capay Valley section presents facts for which guidance is sought ('What advice?'), while the Westlake Village section references an ongoing written advice request. Multiple FPPC opinion letters are attached or referenced. The document is fragmented with scanning/OCR issues in some sections. Primary question is synthetic as no formal Q&A sections are present; conclusion is synthetic as this is a guidance-seeking memo rather than a direct advisory response.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The document addresses two primary legal questions: (1) whether a planning commissioner in Yolo County with substantial agricultural land interests in the Capay Valley has a conflict of interest requiring disqualification from participating in the General Plan update for that region, and (2) guidance on applying the 'public generally' standard to a proposed rent control ordinance in Westlake Village that would affect a specific percentage of housing units.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC memorandum seeks guidance from opinion request meeting participants on how to advise regarding the Capay Valley planning commissioner's potential conflict and how to analyze the 'significant segment of the public' standard in the Westlake Village rent control context, noting that prior opinions have found small percentages (3.25% and less than 1%) to constitute 'significant segments' despite higher percentages in the current matters.

There are 1,069 separate parcels with a total of 397 housing units, 91 of which are nonfarm properties. Thus, approximately 300 of the dwelling units are situated on farms. Of the 1,069 parcels, 860 are zoned agricultural, permitting a minimum lot size of 5 acres; however, very few parcels are less than 20 acres, the minimum size permissible for a dwelling on ag land. The remaining 118 parcels are either government-owned or other industrial/ commercial. The planning commissioner in question owns 160 acres of range land, which he has leased to a tenant. He also has a 1/3 interest in another 900 acres of range land, also leased to this same tenant. Both leases are 1-year leases which have been renewed annually and will be so renewed in the future. He manages and controls 500 acres owned by his father which is leased to another tenant. He also manages and controls another 100 acres owned by his father, which he farms himself. He gets a salary plus a percentage of the profits on this latter parcel. Some of the foregoing land is on the Valley floor, some is hilly and some in the mountains. The thrust of the General Plan proposal is to preserve the agricultural use of the land and to direct residential development into the two small towns (Capay and Guinda). Opinion Request Meeting Participants March 24, 1982 Page Two County Counsel advises that we assume the following: (1) The decisions involving the General Plan will have a material financial effect upon the land owned, managed and controlled by the planning commissioner. (2) The effect upon that land will not be distinguishable from the effect on the "public generally" if the general public is considered to be Capay Valley rather than Yolo County. What advice? I expect to meet with lawyers from "both sides" next week and wish some guidance from you
embedding.summary: This is an internal FPPC memorandum dated March 24, 1982, from Bob Leidlch requesting guidance from opinion request meeting participants on two complex 'public generally' conflict of interest issues: a Yolo County planning commissioner's potential disqualification from General Plan updates affecting his agricultural lands, and the applicability of rent control ordinances to specific housing segments in Westlake Village. The document also includes referenced FPPC advice letters from 1980-1981 regarding conflict of interest standards and the 'material financial effect' test.
